books.title: The Whole Library Seems Fine but You
books.subtitle: For the student sure every classmate is coping with the semester better than they are
books.desc: Everyone else seems to be coasting — pulling the grades, posting the wins, walking into exams like it's nothing — while you're white-knuckling just to keep up. This is a book for the mind that measures its own panicked inside against everyone else's calm outside, and always, always comes up short.
books.long_desc:
Everyone else looks like they've got it handled. The classmate who posts the A. The friend who 'didn't even study' and aced it. The whole feed of people walking into the same semester you're drowning in, somehow dry. You scroll, and you scan the room, and the math always comes out the same: they're fine, and you're the one barely holding on.

This book is for you — the student running a constant, exhausting comparison you didn't sign up for. The one who measures your own racing, bracing, up-all-night inside against everyone else's calm, curated outside, and concludes, every single time, that you're behind. You're not vain and you're not insecure for no reason. You've just got a very precise instrument pointed at the wrong target: yourself, against people who only ever show you their good angle.

You'll come to recognize the comparison for the trap it is — how it weighs your bloopers against their highlight reel, how it treats a single posted grade as proof of a whole life held together, how it gets loudest exactly when you're most tired and most alone with the clock on the wall. You'll see that the people you're measuring yourself against are, a startling amount of the time, bracing just like you — they just don't post the panic. And you'll feel what it's like to put the measuring tape down, even for an afternoon.

This book will not help you win the comparison. It won't make you the calmest one in the room or hand you the routine that finally puts you ahead of everyone else. That's the point. The goal was never to come out on top of a contest you were never really in — it's to stop standing in it at all.

What's here instead is the relief of looking up from the scoreboard. The permission to be exactly as far along as you are, on your own page, in your own time, without the running tally of everyone who looks like they're doing it better. You don't have to win. You just have to stop keeping score.

You're allowed to set the comparison down. You can come back to your own desk, your own blank first page, and breathe.

Book 6 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: They post the A. You see the curve. You assume you're below it.
books.author: Yara Fenwick

books.title: Nothing Is Wrong But Your Whole Body Disagrees
books.subtitle: For the student whose alarm goes off before the exam, before the email, before anything has actually happened — and won't believe there's no fire
books.desc: You're sitting at your desk and there is no real danger, but your heart is sprinting and your stomach has dropped like the floor gave out. This is a book about the alarm that fires when nothing is actually wrong — and about learning to recognize a false alarm for what it is, instead of believing it every single time.
books.long_desc:
You open the laptop to start the reading and your body braces like something is about to hit. Heart going. Hands cold. That low hum under your ribs that says get ready, get ready — for what, you couldn't say. The exam isn't until Thursday. The email hasn't even been sent. Nothing is wrong. But your whole body disagrees, and it's very, very loud.

This book is for you — the college student whose alarm goes off before there's any fire. The one who walks into a normal Tuesday already flinching. You're not dramatic and you're not broken. You're carrying a brilliant, ancient threat-detector that was built for lions and has decided that a syllabus, a group project, and an unread text all qualify.

You'll come to recognize the false alarm in all the places it hides. The 2am jolt awake when there's nothing to do but lie there. The dread that arrives before you've even checked the grade. The way your body insists the danger is right now when the deadline is still days out. You'll feel it most in the exam hall itself — the held breath before the first question, the heart going while you stare at the blue book and the clock on the wall. None of it means you're in danger. It means the alarm is sensitive — and once you can see it firing, you stop having to believe it every single time.

This book will not teach you to silence the alarm forever. It won't optimize you, hand you a morning routine that finally fixes everything, or talk you out of having a body. There is no fixing here, because you were never broken. What's here is recognition: the slow, steadying relief of naming the thing while it's happening, of sitting at the desk and saying — out loud if you need to — this is a false alarm, and I am not actually in danger.

You are allowed to feel the surge and not obey it. You are allowed to let your body be wrong about the lion. You can take one deep breath at the desk, and then the next one, and let the alarm ring itself out while you stay exactly where you are.

Book 1 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: The test is Thursday. Your body thinks it's now.
books.author: Yara Fenwick

books.title: The Version of College You Don't Get to Have
books.subtitle: For the student grieving what was lost — the easier years, the certain plan, the person you were before the anxiety arrived
books.desc: Some of your anxiety isn't fear of what's coming — it's grief for what's already gone: the easier version of yourself, the plan that felt certain, the college experience you pictured and didn't get. This is a book about the loss tangled inside the worry, and about what it means to finally let go of a version of things that was never going to be.
books.long_desc:
There's a version of this you were supposed to have. The college years everyone promised — lighter, surer, the ones where you knew what you were doing and who you were going to be. And somewhere along the way, between the deadlines and the dread and whatever else this season took from you, that version quietly stopped being possible. You don't always have words for it. But some of the weight you're carrying isn't fear of what's ahead. It's grief for what's already gone.

This book is for you — the student mourning something you can't always name. Maybe it's the self you were before the anxiety moved in. Maybe it's a plan that fell through, a relationship that ended, a year you lost, a future you'd already decorated in your head. Maybe it's just the easier person you assumed you'd get to keep being. Whatever it is, you've been treating the ache like a problem to fix, when it was never that. It's a loss to be felt.

You'll come to recognize the grief threaded through the worry — how anxiety and mourning can wear the same face, how bracing for the future is sometimes just refusing to let go of the past, how you can be homesick for a version of your life that never actually arrived. You'll see why no amount of trying harder closes the gap, because the gap is not a failure. It's a loss, and a loss is not something you get past on a deadline. It's something you grieve.

This book will not promise to give the lost thing back, or hand you five steps to 'move on,' or reframe your way around the ache. There is no fix here, because grief was never broken. What's here is the permission to set down a version of things that was never going to be — gently, and in your own time, the way you'd close the last page of a long semester and just sit with the quiet of it being over.

You are allowed to miss who you were. You are allowed to mourn the plan that didn't happen, even while you keep walking toward the one that will. You can let it go without pretending it didn't matter. You can breathe.

Book 5 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: You're not just anxious. You're mourning who you were before.
books.author: Yara Fenwick
keywords: mourning who you used to be

books.title: Everything Is Due and You Have No Room Left
books.subtitle: For the student buried under every deadline at once — when the to-do list is a wall and there's no space left to even think
books.desc: Five things are due, three of them tomorrow, and the moment you try to start one your mind whites out from the size of all the others. This is a book about the flood — too much arriving at once — and about finding a single square foot of room to stand in when your capacity is already gone.
books.long_desc:
It all came at once. The midterm, the lab report, the shift you picked up, the group chat you've stopped opening, the form that was due yesterday. You sit down to start and your mind doesn't pick a task — it tries to hold all of them at the same time, and the whole thing just whites out. Too much. There is no room left to even think.

This book is for you — the student standing at the bottom of a week that arrived all on one day. The one who isn't lazy and isn't behind because you don't care; you're behind because the volume of it exceeded the size of you, and no amount of staring at the list makes the list any shorter.

You'll come to recognize overwhelm as its own distinct thing — not the same as being busy, not the same as being disorganized. It's the flood. It's the moment your capacity is simply exceeded. You'll see how it freezes you precisely when you most need to move, how the size of everything collapses into a single immovable weight on your chest, how 'just make a schedule' is useless advice to someone who has no floor left to build one on. And you'll feel what it's like to set the whole semester down for a moment — not abandon it, just put it on the desk and step back far enough to take one full breath.

This book will not hand you a system to do more in less time. It will not teach you to power through, push harder, or squeeze the week into a tighter shape. The problem was never that you handle pressure badly. The problem is that too much landed at once, and you are a person, not a container with infinite room.

What's here instead is space. The relief of clearing one square foot to stand in. The permission to do the next single thing and let the rest stay where it is, no longer pretending you can carry all of it in your arms at the same time.

You don't have to hold the whole semester right now. You can put it down. You can pick up one corner of it, and only that, and breathe.

Book 2 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: It all landed at the same time, and now there's nowhere to put it.
books.author: Yara Fenwick

books.title: The Manual Everyone Else Seemed to Get
books.subtitle: For the student secretly sure something is wrong with them — overworking to cover a defect they're certain everyone will eventually see
books.desc: It's not that you're behind on an assignment — it's the quiet, constant certainty that something is wrong with you, that everyone else got a manual you missed, and that if you don't work twice as hard they'll find out. This is a book about the shame underneath the anxiety: the hidden defect you've been overworking to cover, and what it's like to finally set the shovel down.
books.long_desc:
Everyone else seems to know how to do this. How to email the professor without rewriting it nine times. How to walk into the seminar like they belong there. How to just be a student, the way the others apparently learned from a manual you never got handed. And underneath the deadlines and the dread, there's a quieter thing you don't say out loud: you're pretty sure the problem isn't the work. The problem is you.

This book is for you — the student who overworks not to excel but to cover, who is convinced that the racing and the bracing are proof of some defect everyone else can see and you can't quite hide. The one who reads a kind comment as pity and a bad grade as confirmation of what you already knew about yourself.

You'll come to recognize shame as the thing sitting under the anxiety — not the same as guilt, which says you did a bad thing, but the older, heavier belief that you ARE the bad thing. You'll see how it hides: behind perfectionism, behind the deleted drafts, behind the way you'd rather be exhausted than exposed. You'll see how it turns every blank first page into a test of your worth instead of just an assignment, and how the overworking was never really about the grade — it was about staying one step ahead of being found out.

This book will not give you a confidence hack or a worksheet that finally makes you feel like enough. It won't talk you into believing you're great or argue you out of how you feel. There's no fix here, because you were never the defect you've been hiding — there is nothing to repair, only something to stop believing.

What's here instead is the slow, disarming relief of being seen and not flinching. The permission to put down the shovel you've been using to bury yourself in work. The quiet recognition that you were never the only one without the manual — almost no one got it, and the ones who look like they did are bracing just like you.

You are allowed to be a person who is still learning, not a defect working overtime to stay hidden. You can set the shovel down. You can breathe.

Book 4 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: You're not behind on the work. You're sure you're behind as a person.
books.author: Yara Fenwick
keywords: something is wrong with me feeling

books.title: One Thought, Then a Thousand, by 3am
books.subtitle: For the student who can't stop the loop — when a single worry splits into a hundred and pulls you down all night long
books.desc: It started with one small worry, and by 3am it has become a thousand — every old embarrassment, every possible failure, every worst-case ending, all looping faster the harder you try to stop. This is a book about the spiral: the downward pull of rumination that feeds on itself, and how to step off it without winning the argument inside your head.
books.long_desc:
It started with one thought. Just one — maybe the way you answered a question in seminar, maybe a grade that wasn't what you wanted. And then it split. One became two, two became ten, and somewhere around 2am you were no longer thinking about the one thing — you were replaying the whole semester, every misstep, every way it could all go wrong, the loop spinning faster the harder you tried to make it stop.

This book is for you — the student who lies in the dark while your mind runs the same circuit a hundred times. The one who knows, intellectually, that this isn't helping, and cannot for the life of you find the exit. You're not overthinking because you're weak-minded. You're caught in a spiral, and a spiral is built to feed itself: every loop kicks up more fuel for the next.

You'll come to recognize the way the spiral works on you — how it disguises rumination as problem-solving, how it promises that if you just think it through one more time you'll finally feel safe, how it always saves its loudest rounds for the moment your defenses are lowest, alone, late, with the clock on the wall ticking toward an exam you'll be too wrecked to sit. You'll see that the spiral is not the truth getting clearer. It's the same thought, dressed in a thousand costumes.

This book will not teach you to win the argument in your head. It won't hand you a thought-stopping trick that makes the loop vanish on command, or promise you a mind that never spirals again. You can't think your way out of a spiral; trying is how it keeps you. There's no fix here, because the loop was never a problem to be solved — it's a pull to step off of.

What's here instead is the quiet of stepping off the loop. The relief of letting a thought be just a thought, of letting it turn without you on it, of one slow breath at a time until the thousand becomes one again, and the one becomes quiet.

You don't have to follow the thought all the way down tonight. You're allowed to let it loop without you. You can set it down and breathe.

Book 3 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: It started with one thought. Now you've replayed the whole semester.
books.author: Yara Fenwick

books.title: The Part of You That Never Looks Away
books.subtitle: For the student whose mind never stops scanning — still reading the room and bracing for the next thing long after the last question is answered
books.desc: Even when nothing is wrong, part of your mind is still on watch — scanning the room, the feed, the future, bracing for a threat that never quite arrives and never quite leaves. This is a book about the watcher: the hypervigilant, always-on part of you that monitors danger long after the danger is gone, and what it costs to never get to rest.
books.long_desc:
The exam ended hours ago. You handed it in. And yet some part of you is still watching — still scanning your own answers, still reading the professor's face, still checking the room, the group chat, the future, for the threat that's surely coming next. It doesn't matter that nothing is wrong right now. The part of you that watches never got the message that the test is over. It will not look away.

This book is for you — the student with a quiet, tireless lookout in the back of your mind that never lowers its guard. The one who walks into every room already finding the exits, who reads a one-word reply seven different ways, who can't settle after the thing is done because part of you is already braced for the next thing. You're not paranoid and you're not high-strung for no reason. You've got a watcher that learned, somewhere, that the only way to be safe is to never stop looking.

You'll come to recognize the watcher as its own distinct kind of anxiety — not the sudden spike of panic, not the flood of overwhelm, but the low, constant, exhausting hum of vigilance that never gets to rest. You'll see how it disguises itself as being careful, as being responsible, as just paying attention. You'll see what it costs: the way you can never fully arrive in the quiet after the last question, because the watcher is already out ahead of you, scanning the horizon for weather that isn't there.

This book will not teach you to silence the watcher or override it by force. It won't promise a technique that finally lets your guard drop on command. There's no fix here, because the watcher was never a malfunction — it was a part of you trying, in its tireless way, to keep you safe. What's here is the slow relief of letting it know, gently, that it's allowed to rest. That the danger it's scanning for is not coming. That it can finally, for a moment, let its shoulders down.

You are allowed to stop scanning. You are allowed to let the test be over. You can sit in the quiet after the last question, let the watcher ease its grip, and breathe.

Book 7 of the Blue Exam Press anxiety series.
books.angle: The exam ended hours ago. The scanning didn't.
books.author: Yara Fenwick
